schools.summary: Paul, identifying himself with the strong (those of robust faith and full liberty), lays down their duty — to bear (support, endure patiently) the infirmities of the weak, not to please themselves; the strong are not to consult their own gratification but to deny themselves for the weaker brother's good (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): strength is given not for self-pleasing but for service; the strong owe it to the weak to support them, accommodating themselves to their infirmity in love.

themes: We then that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak
not to please ourselves
the duty of the strong to bear with the weak
self-denial in love

schools.summary: Paul directs each to please (seek the satisfaction and good of) his neighbour — not in flattery or sin, but 'for his good,' with a view to his edification (building up); the pleasing qualified by the brother's true good, the standard being edification (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): we are to accommodate ourselves to others, yet always toward their edification, not mere man-pleasing — love seeks the neighbour's profit.

themes: Let every one of us please his neighbour
for his good to edification
pleasing the neighbour for his good
self-accommodation aimed at edification

schools.summary: Paul grounds the duty of self-denial in Christ's own example — Christ, the highest, did not please himself but sought the Father's will and our salvation; proved by Psalm 69:9 (a messianic psalm): the reproaches against God fell upon Christ, who bore the insults aimed at God for our sake (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): if Christ, the Lord of all, denied himself so far as to bear reproach for others, how much more should we, his servants, forgo our own pleasure for our brethren.

themes: even Christ pleased not himself
The reproaches of them that reproached thee fell on me
Christ the supreme example of self-denial
bearing reproach for others

schools.summary: All the Old Testament Scriptures were written for our instruction — designed by God for all later generations, not only the age that first received them; that through the patience and consolation the Scriptures supply (by their promises and examples sustaining the believer under trial) we might have hope, the nourishing of the hope of eternal life being their special use (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke).

Calvin: the special end of Scripture is to nourish hope; this passage sharply condemns the fanatics who vaunt that the Old Testament is abolished and belongs not to Christians, for Paul testifies it was appointed by God for our salvation — the same being true of the apostolic writings.

themes: whatsoever things were written aforetime were written for our learning
that we through patience and comfort of the scriptures might have hope
the abiding profit of the Scriptures
Scripture nourishing hope

schools.summary: Paul names God as the author and giver of the patience and consolation just mentioned (v.4) and prays for unity of mind among them — that the strong and weak be of one accord, harmonious in spirit despite their differences over meats and days (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): the likemindedness is to be 'according to Christ Jesus' — patterned on his example and will, in conformity to him, not a mere worldly agreement; harmony among brethren being God's gift, sought in prayer.

themes: the God of patience and consolation grant you to be likeminded
according to Christ Jesus
the prayer for likemindedness
Christian unity God's gift

schools.summary: The purpose of the likemindedness (v.5): that with one accord (one mind) and one voice (one mouth) they may glorify God — unanimous, harmonious praise, the fruit of unity; God glorified as 'the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ,' the distinctive Christian designation (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril: the end of all forbearance and unity among brethren is the common, harmonious glorifying of God — divisions mar his praise, concord magnifies it.

themes: with one mind and one mouth glorify God
even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
the goal of united worship
concord for the glory of God

schools.summary: Paul sums up the exhortation — the strong and weak are to receive (welcome in fellowship) one another — by the measure of Christ's own reception of us: graciously, freely, while we were unworthy, Jew and Gentile alike; Christ's welcome of the undeserving the model and motive for ours (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): Christ received us to the glory of God — mutual acceptance, like Christ's, redounds to God's praise; he gathered Jew and Gentile into one flock under one shepherd.

themes: receive ye one another
as Christ also received us to the glory of God
the command to receive one another
Christ's reception the pattern

schools.summary: Christ exercised his earthly ministry among the Jews ('the circumcision'), born a Jew and sent to them, to vindicate the truth (faithfulness) of God by confirming and fulfilling the promises made to the patriarchs; his coming proved God true to his covenant word (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

Calvin: since Christ fulfilled the promises made to the fathers, the salvation he brought belonged by covenant to the Jews, and the old covenant was therefore spiritual, not merely temporal — against those who reduce the Old Testament promises to earthly things.

themes: Jesus Christ was a minister of the circumcision for the truth of God
to confirm the promises made unto the fathers
Christ confirming the promises
God's faithfulness to his covenant

schools.summary: In contrast with v.8 (Christ to the Jews for God's truth), Christ came to the Gentiles for God's mercy — the Gentiles, having no covenant promises, saved by pure mercy and so glorifying God for it; Paul begins a chain of OT quotations (Psalm 18:49 first) showing the Gentiles' praise of God foretold (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret (with Chrysostom): the Gentiles owe their salvation to mercy alone, not promise — a ground for humility, and a reason to bear with the weak Jews who had the promises.

themes: the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy
I will confess to thee among the Gentiles, and sing unto thy name
the Gentiles glorifying God for mercy
the calling of the nations foretold

schools.summary: The second quotation (Deuteronomy 32:43) — 'Rejoice, ye Gentiles, with his people' — calls the Gentiles to rejoice together with God's people Israel, foretelling their joint participation in salvation; the two joined in one people sharing the same joy (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the prophecy foretells the union of Jew and Gentile in common rejoicing — a ground for their mutual reception and concord.

themes: Rejoice, ye Gentiles, with his people
the Gentiles called to rejoice with God's people
the foretold union of Jew and Gentile
common joy in salvation

schools.summary: The third quotation (Psalm 117:1) — 'Praise the Lord, all ye Gentiles; and laud him, all ye people' — is a universal call to all nations to praise God; the repetition underscoring the breadth, every nation summoned to praise, the gospel embracing all peoples (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret (with Calvin): the prophets repeatedly foretold the Gentiles' calling to praise God — the accumulation of witnesses putting the matter beyond doubt.

themes: Praise the Lord, all ye Gentiles; and laud him, all ye people
the universal call of the Gentiles to praise
the breadth of the gospel
abundant scriptural witness

schools.summary: The climactic quotation (Isaiah 11:10) — the 'root of Jesse' is the Messiah, Christ sprung from David's line — who would rise (in resurrection and exaltation) to reign over the Gentiles, his kingdom extending to all nations, and in whom the Gentiles would trust (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the Gentiles would hope in (trust, believe on) Christ — the object of their faith and the ground of their salvation; the root of Jesse reigning over the believing nations.

themes: There shall be a root of Jesse, and he that shall rise to reign over the Gentiles
in him shall the Gentiles trust
the root of Jesse reigning over the Gentiles
Christ's universal kingdom

schools.summary: Paul calls God 'the God of hope' (the author and giver of hope, fitly named after the prophecies of the Gentiles' hope) and prays that God would fill them with all joy and peace 'in believing' — faith the means by which these blessings are received — that they may abound in hope through the power of the Holy Ghost (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): the joy and peace overflow into abounding hope, all wrought by the power of the Holy Spirit — hope is no human achievement but the Spirit's work; where this hope abounds, the blessed Spirit has all the glory.

themes: Now the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing
that ye may abound in hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost
the prayer to the God of hope
joy, peace, and hope by the Spirit

schools.summary: Paul, having written plainly, expresses his good confidence in them — a gracious courtesy assuring them he does not think them ignorant or evil — crediting them with goodness (benevolence) and knowledge (understanding of the truth), and competent to admonish (counsel) one another (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul's commendation is both sincere and tactful — they are able to admonish one another, his praise preparing them to receive his exhortation graciously.

themes: I myself also am persuaded of you, my brethren
full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, able also to admonish one another
Paul's confidence in the believers
gracious esteem and tact

schools.summary: Paul acknowledges he has written boldly (freely, plainly) on some points, but only 'as putting them in mind' (reminding them of what they already knew), not teaching the ignorant; grounding his boldness in the grace (the apostleship, the office and commission) given him by God (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the humility joined to authority — Paul writes boldly, yet modestly, attributing his right not to himself but to God's grace.

themes: I have written the more boldly unto you in some sort
because of the grace that is given to me of God
Paul's bold writing
apostolic authority humbly exercised

schools.summary: Paul describes his apostleship in priestly terms drawn from temple service (leitourgon, ierourgounta): he ministers the gospel to the Gentiles, who are themselves the offering he presents to God, consecrated by the Holy Spirit as an acceptable sacrifice — but the priesthood is metaphorical, not sacerdotal; the purely figurative use does not make the ministry a literal priesthood (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: this is the priesthood of the Christian pastor — to sacrifice men to God by bringing them to obey the gospel, NOT (as the Papists haughtily vaunt) by offering up Christ to reconcile men to God; the gospel is the sword by which the minister sacrifices men as living victims, sanctified by the Spirit (cf. 12:1).

Clarke: notes Whitby's view of the plain allusion to the Jewish sacrifices — the converted Gentiles the oblation, the Holy Spirit the libation that sanctifies and renders the offering acceptable; still a figure of the apostolic work.

debates.question: Is Paul's 'priesthood' here literal/sacerdotal or metaphorical?

sides.position: Metaphorical — Paul figuratively offers converts to God by bringing them to obey the gospel; not a literal sacrificing priesthood (against the Romish view)
A sacrificial allusion (Whitby) — the Gentiles the oblation, the Spirit the libation — yet still as a figure of the apostolic work

themes: the minister of Jesus Christ to the Gentiles
that the offering up of the Gentiles might be acceptable, being sanctified by the Holy Ghost
Paul's priestly ministry
the offering of the Gentiles

schools.summary: Paul claims a ground of glorying (boasting, exulting) in his apostolic work and its fruit among the Gentiles — a legitimate holy boast — but 'through Jesus Christ,' not in himself or his own merit, his boast wholly referred to Christ who wrought all by him, and confined to the things which pertain to God (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the glorying is through Christ and in the sacred things entrusted to him — not worldly things; the holy boast of the faithful minister ascribing all to Christ.

themes: I have therefore whereof I may glory through Jesus Christ
in those things which pertain to God
Paul's glorying through Christ
the holy boast in the things of God

schools.summary: Paul will not presume to speak of (claim credit for) anything except what Christ actually accomplished through him — ascribing nothing to himself, only to Christ's working; the end being the obedience of the Gentiles (bringing them to the obedience of faith), wrought by word and deed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Christ wrought through Paul both by word (his preaching) and deed (his miracles and labours) — the whole effective ministry being Christ's work, not Paul's.

themes: I will not dare to speak of any of those things which Christ hath not wrought by me
to make the Gentiles obedient, by word and deed
speaking only of Christ's work
all fruit ascribed to Christ

schools.summary: Christ wrought through Paul by mighty signs and wonders (miracles) and the power of the Holy Spirit — the supernatural attestation and inward power accompanying his preaching — over a vast reach, from Jerusalem in a wide circuit as far as Illyricum, the gospel fully preached throughout (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul had fully (thoroughly) preached the gospel throughout the region — not exhaustively to every soul, but planting the gospel everywhere through it.

themes: Through mighty signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God
from Jerusalem, and round about unto Illyricum, I have fully preached the gospel
Paul's mighty ministry
the power and extent of the work

schools.summary: Paul made it his aim (ambition, earnest striving) to preach the gospel where Christ had not yet been named — in regions untouched by the gospel, breaking new ground — not to build on another man's foundation, not labouring where others had planted but pioneering to the unreached (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret (with Calvin): Paul's special calling was to break new ground among the Gentiles, extending the gospel to fresh regions rather than reaping where others had sown.

themes: so have I strived to preach the gospel, not where Christ was named
lest I should build upon another man's foundation
Paul's policy of pioneer preaching
pioneering zeal for the unreached

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:15 — those to whom Christ was not announced shall see, and those who had not heard shall understand, the gospel reaching the previously unreached; Paul applies it to his own pioneer mission, his preaching to the unreached fulfilling the prophecy (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the calling of the unreached Gentiles was foretold, and Paul's pioneer labours are the fulfilment of Isaiah's word — the divine sanction for his strategy.

themes: To whom he was not spoken of, they shall see
they that have not heard shall understand
the prophetic ground of Paul's pioneer mission
the foretold reach of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul explains why he had not yet come to Rome: his pioneer labours in the East (vv.19-21) had so occupied him that he was much hindered (repeatedly prevented) from coming — he had often desired and intended to come, but his work elsewhere prevented it, not from neglect but the press of his unfinished mission (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the hindrance was not from lack of love but from the demands of his calling — he longed to come but was detained by his pioneer work.

themes: I have been much hindered from coming to you
Paul's long hindrance from visiting Rome
the press of his missionary calling
longing for the Roman church

schools.summary: Paul, having 'no more place in these parts' (the eastern regions now evangelized, his pioneer work there complete), is at last free to turn westward; and he had cherished for many years a strong desire to visit Rome, now about to be fulfilled (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the apostle's long-held longing for the Roman believers, now that his eastern labours freed him, was about to be realized.

themes: having no more place in these parts
having a great desire these many years to come unto you
Paul's freedom and long desire
longing now near fulfilment

schools.summary: Paul intended to visit Rome en route to Spain — extending his pioneer mission to the far west, with Rome as a stopping-point — hoping the Roman church would help him forward (assist, equip, accompany) on his journey, a partnership in the mission (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret (with Calvin): Paul desired first to enjoy their fellowship, to be satisfied in some measure with their company — a warm expression of his longing for mutual refreshment.

themes: Whensoever I take my journey into Spain, I will come to you
to be brought on my way thitherward by you
Paul's plan to visit Rome on the way to Spain
missionary ambition joined to affection

schools.summary: Before going to Rome and Spain, Paul must first go to Jerusalem to minister (serve) the saints there, by delivering the collection gathered for the poor believers; his present task a ministry of relief, carrying the contribution from the Gentile churches to the needy saints (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): this errand of mercy, uniting Gentile and Jewish believers in practical love, was a priority Paul would not defer even for his longed-for visit to Rome.

themes: now I go unto Jerusalem to minister unto the saints
Paul's present errand to Jerusalem
the ministry of relief
mercy uniting the churches

schools.summary: The churches of Macedonia and Achaia (the Greek provinces) had been pleased (freely chose) to make a contribution (collection of money) for the poor saints at Jerusalem, who were in poverty (from persecution or famine); the Gentile churches gathering relief for them (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the contribution was a free and glad gift — 'it pleased them' — the Gentiles cheerfully aiding their Jewish brethren in need.

themes: them of Macedonia and Achaia to make a certain contribution
for the poor saints which are at Jerusalem
the Gentile churches' contribution
glad relief of the Jewish poor

schools.summary: Though the gift was free, it was also a debt — the Gentiles owed the Jews this material help; for the gospel and all spiritual blessings came to the Gentiles from the Jews (the oracles, the Messiah, the apostles), so having received spiritual riches they fittingly repay in carnal (material) things (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Wesley (with Theodoret): the equity — it is just that those who received the greater (spiritual) blessings should minister the lesser (material); a fair and grateful exchange.

themes: their debtors they are
if the Gentiles have been made partakers of their spiritual things, their duty is also to minister unto them in carnal things
the Gentiles' debt to the Jews
just reciprocity between the churches

schools.summary: When Paul has performed the errand (delivered the collection) and 'sealed to them this fruit' (securely conveyed the gift, the fruit of the Gentiles' love, as a sealed and certified deliverance), he will come by way of Rome to Spain (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): only after faithfully completing this ministry of mercy would Paul turn to Rome and Spain — duty before desire.

themes: when I have performed this, and have sealed to them this fruit
I will come by you into Spain
Paul's resolve to come after delivering the collection
duty before desire

schools.summary: Paul holds a settled assurance about his coming visit — confident he will come 'in the fulness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ,' laden with abundant gospel blessing, a full measure of the spiritual benefits Christ imparts, to be shared with the Roman church (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul, conscious of his Spirit-empowered ministry, was sure his coming would be richly fruitful — bringing the fulness of Christ's blessing for their mutual edification.

themes: I am sure that, when I come unto you, I shall come in the fulness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ
Paul's assurance of gospel blessing
confidence of a fruitful visit
abundant blessing to be shared

schools.summary: Paul earnestly entreats them by the strongest motives — for Christ's sake and by the love which the Spirit produces — to 'strive together' (sunagonizomai, to wrestle, agonize, labour earnestly alongside him) in prayer to God for him; fervent, striving prayer as in a struggle (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): the humility of the great apostle, conscious of dangers ahead, humbly seeking the prayers of the Roman believers — the strong needing the intercession of others; agonizing intercession of real value.

themes: I beseech you, for the Lord Jesus Christ's sake, and for the love of the Spirit
that ye strive together with me in your prayers to God for me
Paul's request for striving prayer
the value of united intercession

schools.summary: Paul asks prayer for deliverance from the unbelieving Jews in Judaea, who were hostile and posed real danger to his life (as the event proved, Acts 21); and that his service (the delivery of the collection) be well received by the Jerusalem believers, graciously accepted not rejected through prejudice (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul foresaw both the hostility of the unbelievers and the possible suspicion of the believers toward the Gentile gift, and sought prayer for both perils.

themes: That I may be delivered from them that do not believe in Judaea
that my service which I have for Jerusalem may be accepted of the saints
the two-fold object of prayer
foresight of the perils

schools.summary: The ultimate aim of these prayers: that Paul, delivered and his service accepted, may come to Rome with joy (his arrival glad, not sorrowful or in chains) 'by the will of God' (all his plans subordinated to the divine pleasure), and be refreshed together with them in fellowship (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Theodoret): Paul desires to be refreshed (find rest and joy) together with the Roman believers — a mutual refreshment in Christian fellowship.

themes: That I may come unto you with joy by the will of God
may with you be refreshed
the goal of a joyful arrival
mutual refreshment in fellowship

schools.summary: Paul closes with a characteristic benediction, naming God as 'the God of peace' — the author and giver of peace, fitly invoked after a chapter much concerned with concord among brethren — praying that this God would be with them all, his presence the source of reconciliation and harmony, 'all' embracing both strong and weak (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: after exhorting to mutual forbearance and unity, Paul commends them to the God of peace, whose presence would secure the concord he had urged.

themes: Now the God of peace be with you all. Amen
the benediction of the God of peace
God the securer of concord
peace on the whole church
